Site: Fimiston
metadata: Fimiston Open Pit Mine is the largest open cut gold mine in Australia. The gold within this system is unusual in that it is present as telluride minerals within pyrite. In order to recover the gold, the ore must be crushed, passed through a gravity circuit to recover the free gold, and then subjected to flotation to produce an auriferous pyrite-telluride concentrate. The pit, currently owned by Northern Star Resources, is over 3.5 kilometers long, some 1.5 kilometers wide and over 600 meters deep. The site is located directly east of the town of Kalgoorlie.

Site: Quebrada Blanca Mine, Collahuasi mining district, Pica, Tamarugal Province, Tarapacá
metadata: Quebrada Blanca is a copper mine located in the Atacama Desert of northern Chile at an elevation of 4’400 meters, approximately 1’500 kilometers from Santiago. Copper concentrate processed at the site is transported to the port facility for loading onto ship for transport to market. Quebrada Blanca is majority owned by Teck Resources and uses desalinated seawater for all production processes. In 2023 the mine produced 64’300 tons of copper.

Site: Balmertown area, Kenora District, Ontario
metadata: Balmertown is a community within the Municipality of Red Lake, located in the Red Lake gold district, Kenora District, Ontario, Canada, on the traditional lands of the Wabauskang and Lac Seul First Nations. It's part of a region known for its high-grade gold deposits, particularly within the Balmer Assemblage of Archean rocks. The Red Lake gold district  and its underground mines are being restored and expanded in 2025.

Site: Coleman, Crows Nest Pass, Alberta
metadata: In the early 20th century, the Crowsnest Pass Coal mines in the Canadian Rockies were home to languages and cultures of European immigrants. Through the years, coal mining suffered from fluctuating coal prices, bitter strikes, and underground accidents. The mines are now silent symbols of generations of workers and their families, and the changing fortunes of the mining industry. A recent study found that mountaintop removal coal mining such as that practiced in Crowsnest increases the discharge of mine effluent to rivers, underscoring the lasting downstream impacts of abandoned and even reclaimed coal mines.

Site: Cleveland County, North Carolina
metadata: The Kings Mountain Mine is a large open-pit mine with over 100 different mineral specimens on the south side of Kings Mountain in Cleveland County, North Carolina. Its open-pit has been excavated in one of the largest bedrock lithium deposits in the United States. Albemarle has started the permitting process to resume open pit mining and expand the old gold mine,idled in the early 1990s.

Site: Échassières, Vichy, Allier, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes
metadata: The history of the Beauvoir quarry reaches all the way back to the bronze age. In the 19th century the mineral Kaolin was first found on site and used for ceramics. Kaolin mining was industrialized in the early 21st century. Already in the 1960s granite bearing lithium was identified, and more recently field studies found economically significant deposits of lithium in the mine area.

Site: Kalongwe deposit, Mutshatsha, Lualaba
metadata: The Kalongwe copper-cobalt project, located near Mutshatsha about 60 km southwest of Kolwezi in Lualaba Province, DRC, lies on the western Central African Copper Belt and hosts deeply weathered, sediment-hosted, high-grade oxide mineralization. As of April 2018, it contained 7.99 Mt of proven and probable reserves grading 2.94% copper and 0.34% cobalt. Conventional open-pit mining with drilling, blasting, and truck-shovel operations will be employed, using 90 t excavators, 40 t articulated dump trucks, and a top-hammer drill rig, positioning Kalongwe as a significant source of near-surface copper and cobalt resources.

Site: Lualaba
metadata: The Kalongwe copper and cobalt project, located near Mutshatsha about 60 kilometres southwest of Kolwezi in Lualaba Province of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, lies on the western Central African Copper Belt and hosts deeply weathered, sediment-hosted, high-grade oxide mineralisation. As of April 2018, it contained 7.99 million tonnes of proven and probable reserves grading 2.94 percent copper and 0.34 percent cobalt. Conventional open-pit mining with drilling, blasting, and truck-shovel operations will be employed, using 90-tonne excavators, 40-tonne articulated dump trucks, and a top-hammer drill rig, making Kalongwe a significant source of near-surface copper and cobalt resources.

Site: Kolwezi, Mutshatsha, Lualaba
metadata: Kolwezi, in Mutshatsha Territory of Lualaba Province in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, is a global hub for copper and cobalt extraction. Situated near the Zilo Gorges of the Lualaba River on the Lubumbashi–Lobito transport corridor, it evolved from precolonial artisanal mining to large-scale industrial operations initiated by Belgian interests in the early twentieth century. Its landscape is now dominated by vast mechanised pits producing huge volumes of copper and cobalt. A 2021 Guardian report notes that Chinese companies control most of these mines, where Congolese workers report exploitation, discrimination, and racism under the new management.

Site: Bernic Lake, Lac-du-Bonnet, Manitoba
metadata: The Tanco Mine at Bernic Lake in the Lac du Bonnet area of Manitoba, Canada, is a world-class source of tantalum, lithium and caesium from the Tanco Pegmatite. This 2.67-billion-year-old, highly differentiated and strongly zoned pegmatite was first discovered in 1930 and later mined for tin, beryl, lithium, tantalum and caesium at various periods. Its pollucite deposit, the largest known globally, contains about 350,000 tons of caesium—roughly two-thirds of the world’s identified resources. Operations passed from The Tantalum Mining Corporation of Canada to Cabot Corporation in 1993 and to Sinomine in 2019, which continues caesium brine production.

Site: Kings Mountain, North Carolina
metadata: The Foote Lithium Co. Mine is nestled in Kings Mountain in Cleveland County, North Carolina. This location is part of the Kings Mountain Mining District, historically significant for minerals like gold, lithium, and mica.  The mine is renowned for its lithium, extracted mainly from spodumene, and it boasts an impressive array of over 160 valid minerals, with 16 being type localities. It is famous for a variety of rare phosphates and silicates. The Foote Mine and its operations are now under the ownership of the Albemarle Corporation.

Site: Aletai Prefecture, Xinjiang
metadata: The Koktokay No. 3 pegmatite in Xinjiang, China, is a highly evolved lithium-beryllium-niobium-tantalum-cesium granitic pegmatite of the spodumene subtype and the largest rare-metal deposit in the Chinese Altai orogenic belt. It displays a distinctive concentric zonation with nine mineralogical-textural zones from a graphic pegmatite rim to a quartz-microcline core. Its cupola-shaped section measures about 250 meters in each dimension. Historically worked for pollucite, spodumene, lepidolite, beryl and columbite-tantalite, the deposit exemplifies an exceptional pegmatitic system with significant rare-metal resources and a well-preserved internal structure.

Site: Buranga pegmatite, Muhororo, Ngororero District, Western Province
metadata: The Buranga pegmatite in Muhororo, Ngororero District, Western Province of Rwanda, is an unusual aluminium-rich and iron-manganese-rich phosphate pegmatite within a region known for lithium-caesium-tantalum deposits. Set amid the scenic hills of western Rwanda, it represents an important source of strategic minerals. Ongoing work focuses on defining resources and improving recovery rates of lithium, kaolin, silica and mica as part of an expanding feasibility study, highlighting the deposit’s potential for contributing to the country’s growing rare-metal and industrial mineral sector.

Site: Aidyrlya River, Orenburg Oblast
metadata: The Aidyrlya gold deposit, situated along the Aidyrlya River in Orenburg Oblast, Russia, lies within the Ural River basin via its tributary, the Suunduk River. This locality is recognised for its diverse mineralogy, including members of the serpentine subgroup, and is notable for exploitable or exploited commodities such as gold and tungsten. Its geological setting and resource profile make it an important site for studying mineralisation patterns and evaluating the economic potential of precious and strategic metals within the region.

Site: Mirny Mine, Mirny, Mirninsky District, Sakha
metadata: The Mirny Mine in Mirny, Sakha, Russia, is a diamond-producing kimberlite deposit originally developed as a 525-meter-deep open pit with a diameter of 1,200 meters, making it the second-largest excavated hole in the world. Discovered in 1955, open-pit operations ceased in 2001, after which mining continued underground. By 1999, all production was underground, with a current capacity of 500 thousand tons of ore annually and a design capacity of one million tons per year. The mine’s projected lifespan extends to 2043, supporting its status as a significant source of diamonds in Russia.

Site: Gash Barka Region
metadata: The Bisha Mine, located 150 kilometres west of Asmara in Eritrea’s Gash Barka Region, is a large open-pit volcanic-associated massive sulfide deposit producing copper, zinc, gold, and silver. Covering a licensed mining and exploration area of 110 square kilometres, it contains reserves of 3.09 million tonnes of zinc and 800,000 tonnes of copper. The project is operated jointly by Zijin Mining, holding 55 percent, and Eritrean National Mining, holding 45 percent. Its high-grade ore and strategic metal content make Bisha a key contributor to Eritrea’s mining sector and regional mineral production.

Site: Arlit, Agadez
metadata: The Imouraren uranium mine, located about 80 kilometres south of Arlit in Niger’s Agadez Region, hosts one of the country’s largest uranium reserves, with 109.1 million tonnes of ore grading 0.06 percent uranium. The sandstone-hosted deposit was developed by French company Areva and SOPaMin on behalf of the Nigerien government. Excavations began in 2012 following a 2009 operating permit but were suspended in 2015 due to low uranium prices. In 2023, Orano explored in-situ recovery methods for extraction, raising concerns over potential impacts on local water resources.

Site: Bozhou District, Zunyi, Guizhou
metadata: The Houcao bauxite deposit in Bozhou District, Zunyi, Guizhou, China, forms part of the Lower Carboniferous Jiujialu Formation and consists of thick, cyclic layers of bauxite ore and bauxitic claystone. Developed through intense chemical weathering and leaching, the deposit is rich in aluminum oxide and the mineral diaspore, with low silica content, reflecting formation in a well-drained environment. As the primary source of metallic aluminum, bauxite from Houcao is a vital strategic mineral, essential for aluminum production, supporting industrial development, and underpinning the supply of this key metal for manufacturing and infrastructure.

Site: Green hill open mine, Tsumeb Mine (Ongopolo Mine), Tsumeb, Oshikoto Region
metadata: The Tsumeb Mine in Namibia is a world-renowned copper, lead, zinc, silver, germanium, and cadmium deposit, noted for its exceptional mineral diversity, with at least 170 species recorded, including 20 unique to the locality. Formation occurred in a non-arid environment with oxygen-rich groundwater, promoting leaching and redeposition of elements into crystalline structures. The nearby metal smelter, historically operated by Namibia Custom Smelters and sold by Weatherly International to Dundee Corporation in 2010, has contributed to significant soil contamination with arsenic and other heavy metals, highlighting both the site’s economic importance and environmental challenges

Site: Koashva Mountain, Murmansk Oblast
metadata: The Koashva Open Pit on Koashva Mountain, Khibiny Massif, Murmansk Oblast, Russia, is a major apatite deposit operated by Apatite Joint-Stock Company, a leading producer of phosphate raw materials for fertiliser. Located near Lake Umbozero, the deposit lies within an alkaline massif featuring ultra-agpaitic pegmatites, urtite, foidolites, apatite-nepheline rocks, and various mineral veins. Discovered in 1959, Koashva hosts a series of lens-shaped ore bodies extending over three kilometres, with thickness decreasing from 200–300 metres near the surface to several metres at depth, making it the richest deposit in the Khibiny and a key resource for phosphate production.

Site: Franklin, Sussex County, New Jersey
metadata: The Buckwheat Pit at the former Franklin Mine in Sussex County, New Jersey, represents a remnant of historic zinc ore extraction. Opened in 1866 in a buckwheat field and later expanded as the Taylor Mine, it operated with an aerial tramway until its closure around 1912, while underground mining nearby persisted until 1954. The site contains zinc and iron ore veins within Franklin Marble, a late Ordovician lamprophyre dike, and vuggy Buckwheat Dolomite Marble of Paleozoic age. These distinctive formations produced an extraordinary range of minerals, including more than one hundred species known to fluoresce under ultraviolet light.

Site: Zheleznogorsk-Ilimsky, Nizhneilimsky District, Irkutsk Oblast
metadata: The Korshunovskoye iron deposit near Zheleznogorsk-Ilimsky in the Nizhneilimsky District of Irkutsk Oblast, Russia, is a notable skarn-type formation. It contains iron-rich mineralization associated with explosive pipes hosted in salt-bearing strata. This geological setting has produced significant concentrations of iron alongside boron-related minerals, reflecting complex processes of metasomatism and hydrothermal activity.

Site: Udachnaya pipe, Daldyn, Mirninsky District, Sakha
metadata: The Udachnaya open-pit mine in the Daldyn area of the Mirninsky District, Sakha Republic, Russia, exploits two intersecting kimberlite pipes intruded into Lower Ordovician limestone. Containing diamond eclogite, dunite, and peridotite xenoliths, the pit extends about 640 metres deep, making it one of the world’s deepest of its type. Discovered in 1955 shortly after the Mir pipe, it forms part of the Daldyn-Alakit kimberlite field. The nearby settlement of Udachny takes its name from this deposit, which holds vast diamond reserves and produces millions of carats annually under the management of the Russian company Alrosa.

Site: Phalaborwa, Ba-Phalaborwa Local Municipality, Mopani District, Limpopo
metadata: Palabora Mine in Phalaborwa, Limpopo, is operated by Palabora Mining Company Limited, established in 1956. It is South Africa’s only active copper producer and the largest employer in Ba-Phalaborwa. Initially an open-pit, it transitioned to underground mining between 2001 and 2004. The site yields copper cathode and rod, as well as nickel sulfate, sulfuric acid, magnetite, vermiculite, precious metals, uranium, zirconium, platinum, and phosphate. The mine lies within the 1,950-hectare Phalaborwa Complex of pyroxenite, foskorite, and sövite carbonatite, containing vast apatite resources and accessory minerals including uraninite, thorianite, and baddeleyite extracted through large-scale operations.

Site: Wyndham-East Kimberley Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Argyle Diamond Mine in the remote East Kimberley region of Western Australia was discovered in 1979 and became the world’s first commercially successful diamond operation exploiting a lamproite pipe. Commencing production in 1983 and transitioning from open-pit to underground mining in 2013, it yielded more than 865 million carats of rough diamonds before ceasing operations in November 2020. Operated by Rio Tinto, Argyle was the largest global producer of natural diamonds by volume and the principal source of pink and red stones, also supplying rare blue, champagne, cognac, near-gem and industrial diamonds over its 37-year lifespan.

Site: La Unión, Murcia
metadata: The San Valentín Mine near La Unión in Murcia, Spain, is a large open-pit operation historically exploited for lead, zinc, and iron ores. Extensive mining and processing of pyrite, sphalerite, galena, and cassiterite from nearby deposits between 1952 and 1981 generated substantial flotation tailings and waste materials. These abandoned deposits, including the Descargador mine pond, have altered the local landscape, disrupted natural drainage, and contributed to widespread soil, water, and ecosystem contamination. The resulting geochemical hazards pose ongoing risks to human health and threaten the Mar Menor coastal lagoon, highlighting the urgent need for environmental remediation.

Site: Moab-Setting Lakes area, Thompson Nickel Belt, Manitoba
metadata: The Thompson Mine in the Moab-Setting Lakes area of the Thompson Nickel Belt in Manitoba, Canada, is an open-pit operation that began in 1986 to extract high-grade nickel sulfide. It is located near the planned city of Thompson, established in 1956 along the Burntwood River, which serves as the largest urban and service hub in northern Manitoba, providing health care, education, retail trade, and transportation links to many remote Indigenous communities. The region, the world’s fifth-largest nickel belt, has yielded billions of pounds of nickel yet remains underexplored, supported by extensive infrastructure and clean hydroelectric power for future low-carbon development.

Site: Mary Kathleen district, Rosebud Station, Cloncurry Shire, Queensland
metadata: The Mary Kathleen Mine in north-western Queensland, Australia, operated east of Mount Isa between 1956 and 1963 and again from 1975 to 1982. Developed by Mary Kathleen Uranium Limited, it produced almost 9,000 tonnes of uranium oxide to supply overseas markets, alongside large reserves of rare earth oxides. An architect-designed township supported about 1,000 residents before both mine and town were dismantled after closure in 1984, marking Australia’s largest auction. The site hosted the country’s first large-scale open-cut rehabilitation program to cover radioactive waste and promote revegetation, although signs of deterioration and leaching are now evident at the former operation.

Site: Sepon Mine, Vilabouly District, Savannakhet Province
metadata: The Khanong Open Pit at the Sepon Mine in the Vilabouly District of Savannakhet Province, Laos, opened in 2003 and comprises three copper pits and eighteen gold pits, most of which are now depleted. Initially focused on extensive gold production, operations shifted to high-grade copper averaging 5.4 percent, with additional pits mined until 2020 before copper output was reduced and gold processing resumed. Owned and operated by MMG, a subsidiary of China Minmetals, the mine is renowned for producing rare azurite and malachite specimens, making it one of the most distinctive mineral locations in south-central Laos.

Site: Tharsis Mines, Tharsis, Alosno, Huelva, Andalusia
metadata: The Filón Sur open pit at the Tharsis Mines in Alosno, Huelva, Andalusia, Spain, forms part of the Tharsis-La Zarza mining basin within the Iberian Pyrite Belt. Exploited since antiquity, the area reached peak production in the 19th and 20th centuries under the British Tharsis Sulphur and Copper Company, which introduced modern extraction techniques and surface mining through the cortas system. The operation supported industrial facilities, railways, and mining towns, establishing a significant mining-industrial complex. Today, the site preserves extensive historical and industrial heritage, with ongoing initiatives promoting conservation and tourism to showcase its legacy within Spain’s mining history.

Site: Reocín, Cantabria
metadata: The Reocín Mine in Cantabria, Spain, is the country’s largest stratabound carbonate-hosted zinc-lead deposit. Located on the Santillana Synclinal border in the Vasco Cantabria basin, about 30 km southeast of Santander, it was closed in 2003. Open-pit operations created a large excavation that gradually filled with water from the Saja River, rain, and minor diapir inflows, forming a controlled mining lake. Water infiltration, averaging 1,100 to 1,299 liters per second, posed significant operational and stability challenges, requiring careful drainage management based on the site’s unique geological and hydrogeological conditions.

Site: Bisbee, Cochise County, Arizona
metadata: The Lavender Pit in Bisbee, Cochise County, Arizona, was an open-pit copper mine operated by Phelps Dodge from 1950 to 1974, producing approximately 600,000 tons of copper from 86 million tons of ore, with gold, silver, and turquoise as by-products. Named after Harrison M. Lavender, the pit replaced the earlier Sacramento Hill mine and features steep sides due to competent host rock. Covering 300 acres and reaching 274 meters deep, it generated 256 million tons of waste, some acid-leached for additional copper. The adjacent Cochise deposit, north of the pit, holds potential for future mining.

Site: Climax Mining District, Lake County, Colorado
metadata: The Climax Mine in Lake County, Colorado, is a major molybdenum operation covering approximately 14,350 acres. Established in 1915, it once supplied three quarters of the world’s molybdenum. After a 17-year closure, the mine reopened in 2012 as a surface open pit with a 25,000-ton-per-day mill capable of producing about 30 million pounds annually. The deposit features multiple shells of molybdenite-bearing stockwork veins within a siliceous rhyolite porphyry stock. The mill also recovers pyrite, wolframite, cassiterite, and monazite. The mine is owned by Climax Molybdenum Company, a subsidiary of Freeport-McMoRan.

Site: Kramer Borate deposit, Boron, Kern County, California
metadata: The Rio Tinto Borax Mine in Boron, California, is the largest open-pit borax operation in the world, producing nearly half of global borates. Discovered in 1913, mining began with shafts in the 1920s, and the current open-pit operation opened in 1957 under U.S. Borax, now part of Rio Tinto Group. Reserves are sufficient for economic production through the early 2040s. The mine is operated by Rio Tinto’s Borax division. In 2019, a pilot project was launched to recover lithium from mining waste, expanding the site’s contribution to mineral production beyond borates.

Site: Almoharín, Cáceres, Extremadura
metadata: The La Parrilla Mine, also known as the Adelaida open pit, is situated in Almoharín, Cáceres, Extremadura, Spain and is the country’s largest tungsten deposit. Currently inactive, it holds substantial reserves estimated at around 30 million tonnes of ore grading 0.11 percent tungsten. The deposit consists of a quartz vein stockwork within metapelitic rocks and contains diverse sulphide minerals including galena, sphalerite, and native silver. With direct highway access and proximity to Atlantic and Mediterranean ports, the site is part of Spain’s strategic projects under the European Union Critical Raw Materials Act, which seeks to meet the Union’s demand for raw materials by 2030.

Site: Girilambone, Canbelego Co., New South Wales
metadata: The Girilambone Copper Mine, including the Murrawombie open pit and West Bogan Mine, in central New South Wales. Copper was discovered in 1875. The site was initially worked with limited success due to metallurgical challenges and inadequate processing technology. Redevelopment introduced gravity concentration and chemical leaching, but large-scale extraction began in the 1990s using innovative heap leaching, solvent extraction, and electrowinning, producing about 400 tonnes of copper annually. The site holds cultural significance for the Weilwan people, with “Girilambone” meaning “place of falling stars” .

Site: Ouenza, Ouenza District, Tébessa Province
metadata: The Ouenza Mine in Tébessa Province, Algeria, is a large open-pit operation extracting iron carbonate deposits from Aptian neritic limestones near Triassic evaporitic diapirs. Producing between 2 and 2.5 million tons of iron ore annually, it contains reserves estimated at 120 to 150 million tons. The ore comprises siderite, hematite, and goethite, which influence its iron content and quality. Together with the Boukhadra Mine, Ouenza was managed by Société des Mines de Fer de l’Est, owned mainly by Manal Group and partly by EPE SIDER El Hadjar S.p.A., highlighting its importance in Algeria’s iron production.

Site: Antofagasta de la Sierra Department, Catamarca Province
metadata: The Tincalayu Mine, situated in the Tincalayu borax deposit at the northern edge of the Salar del Hombre Muerto in Catamarca Province, Argentina, is a major borate producer in South America. It operates two open pits, the largest measuring 1,500 meters wide, 500 meters long, and 100 meters deep, at an altitude of about 4,000 meters above sea level. Formerly exploited by Boroquímica and linked to processing at Campo Quijano, the mine lies within a high-altitude salt pan that also forms part of the Lithium Triangle, a globally significant source of lithium for renewable energy and electric vehicle technologies.

Site: Pirquitas Mine, Mina Pirquitas, Rinconada Department, Jujuy Province
metadata: The Oploca Vein System, on the southern margin of the Pirquitas Mine in Jujuy Province, Argentina, trends east-west and is shifting from underground extraction at the San Miguel shaft to early-stage open-pit operations. The Pirquitas Mine applies conventional drilling, blasting, and grade control techniques to mine approximately 50,000 tonnes per day, feeding a processing plant with three crushing stages capable of 6,000 tonnes per day. Ore is upgraded through a preconcentration jig system. As of September 30, 2011, proven and probable reserves exceeded 15 million tonnes containing substantial silver, tin, and zinc, highlighting the site’s significant polymetallic resources.

Site: Veladero Mine, Frontera mining district, Tudcum, Iglesia Department, San Juan Province
metadata: The Amable Pit is part of the Veladero Mine in the Frontera District of San Juan Province, Argentina, situated at elevations between 4,000 and 4,850 meters above sea level and approximately 374 kilometres northwest of San Juan city. Operated as a 50/50 joint venture with Shandong Gold, Veladero is a large open-pit truck and shovel operation producing gold and silver through heap leaching since 2005 and was the first in the country to meet the Towards Sustainable Mining Standard. Current plans focus on extending operations beyond 2034, supported by intensified exploration of high-sulfidation targets within the district.

Site: Cadia Valley operations (Cadia-Ridgeway Cu-Au deposit), Cadia, Bathurst Co., New South Wales
metadata: Cadia Hill Mine, part of the Cadia Valley operations near Orange in New South Wales, Australia, comprises extensive open-cut and underground gold and copper mines operated by Newmont. Developed since the 1990s, it is the country’s second largest open-cut mine after the Super Pit at Kalgoorlie and a major regional employer with a projected life of several decades. Adjacent Ridgeway and Cadia East underground mines add substantial resources. The operation is a pioneer in block cave mining and uses advanced automation, including remotely orchestrated loaders and auxiliary equipment, to enhance productivity and efficiency.

Site: Brisbane Co., New South Wales
metadata: Bengalla Open Cut Mine near Muswellbrook in New South Wales is a large black coal operation with approval to extract up to 15 million tonnes of run-of-mine coal annually until 2039. Employing over 600 people and operating continuously, it is recognised as a cost-competitive producer within the lowest quartile of global seaborne thermal coal costs. The mining fleet supports a 13.4 million tonne growth project, with the coal handling and preparation plant ramping up after a tailings capacity upgrade. Current efforts focus on extending the mine’s life beyond 2037 and completing new infrastructure.

Site: Brisbane Co., New South Wales
metadata: Mangoola Open Cut Mine in New South Wales is a black coal operation originally developed by Centennial Coal to supply fuel for electricity generation. Approved in 2006 amid environmental debate, it became a landmark case when the Land and Environment Court ruled the government had inadequately assessed greenhouse gas emissions. Centennial later sold the project to Xstrata, which was subsequently acquired by Glencore. Production began in 2011, and in 2021 the mine secured approval to continue operating until 2030, reflecting its ongoing role in regional energy supply and the evolving regulatory landscape for coal mining in Australia.

Site: Brisbane Co., New South Wales
metadata: Mount Pleasant Open Cut Mine near Muswellbrook in New South Wales is a greenfield black coal operation extending over six square kilometres and reaching about 220 metres in depth. Managed by Thiess Mining, it holds an estimated 474 million tonnes of reserves producing high-quality, low-impurity coal for international markets. The operation uses controlled blasting to fragment rock before excavation and rail transport. Recently, the New South Wales Court of Appeal overturned approval for an expansion of this project, finding that the Independent Planning Commission had not adequately assessed the climate impacts of emissions from the combustion of exported coal.

Site: Girilambone, Canbelego Co., New South Wales
metadata: The Murrawombie Open Pit, historically known as the Girilambone Copper Mine in central western New South Wales, lies two kilometres west of Girilambone and was first discovered in 1875. Modern mining began in 1992 under the Girilambone Copper Company, a joint venture later wholly owned by Straits Resources, and produced copper oxide ore through heap leaching, solvent extraction, and electrowinning until 2002. The pit reached about 150 metres in depth within highly foliated meta-sediments hosting a massive sulphide deposit. Residual leaching continues today, recovering around 400 tonnes of copper annually from the remaining heap leach solution as copper cement.

Site: Clarke Co., New South Wales
metadata: Gibsons Open Cut, is a gold mine located approximately 1.25 kilometres south of Halls Peak in Clarke County, New South Wales. Historically, the mine operated through a combination of shafts, adits, and open-cut methods. A November 2024 pre-feasibility study confirmed the Mt Gibson Gold Project as a large-scale operation, projecting annual gold production exceeding 150,000 ounces during the first fifteen years, highlighting its significant potential as a long-term contributor to regional gold output.

Site: Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Glendell Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, was part of the Mt. Owen/Glendell Operation, producing thermal and semi-soft metallurgical coal from seams 0.3 to 3.5 metres thick. Operations ceased in November 2022, while Glencore has submitted a modification application to extend mining north of the Glendell Pit, targeting the Hebden Seam. The proposed Glendell Pit Extension aims to continue coal production, maintaining the site’s role in supplying both thermal and metallurgical coal to domestic and international markets.

Site: Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Liddell Open Cut Mine, located 25 kilometres northwest of Singleton in the Upper Hunter Valley, New South Wales, was a century-old operation producing thermal and metallurgical coal for export. Mining concluded in 2023, initiating the Liddell Coal Closure Project focused on site rehabilitation. A  May 2023 modification to development consent permits ongoing use of the mine’s final voids until 2050 for tailings and water storage. This transition reflects a shift from active extraction to environmental management, ensuring safe closure and continued utility of the site’s remaining infrastructure.

Site: Mount Arthur Coal Mine Complex, Muswellbrook, Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Mt Arthur North Mine, part of the Mount Arthur Coal Complex near Muswellbrook, New South Wales, is an open-cut operation opened by the Broken Hill Proprietary Company in 2002 and the state’s largest coal mine. It has a capacity of 20 million tonnes per year, mining 21 distinct seams and employing 2,000 staff. Coal is transported via Aurizon to the Port of Newcastle for export. Following a 2020 shift to export-only production, BHP considered selling the mine but retained it in 2022, planning closure by 2030. Post-mining, the site may be repurposed for a pumped-storage hydroelectricity project.

Site: Muswellbrook, Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Muswellbrook Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, concluded over a century of coal production in 2022, with final coal transport completed in March 2023. Established by the Muswellbrook Coal Company, the site began underground mining in 1907 and open-cut operations in 1944. Rehabilitation is now underway under Idemitsu Australia, which is implementing productive post-mining land uses, including a pumped-storage hydropower project with AGL and a large-scale solar farm with ESCO Pacific, demonstrating a transition from active mining to sustainable energy-focused land management

Site: Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Ravensworth Open Cut Mine, fully owned by Glencore, is located in the Hunter Valley between Muswellbrook and Singleton, New South Wales. The operation produces thermal and metallurgical coal for export using a fleet of hydraulic shovels, excavators, and rear dump trucks, extracting approximately 13.3 million tonnes of run-of-mine coal and 63 million cubic metres of waste annually. In 2024, Ravensworth employed 760 people and rehabilitated 55 hectares. Ravensworth Underground ceased operations in 2014, entering care and maintenance, and was officially sealed in 2023 with surface infrastructure decommissioned, marking a complete transition to open-cut mining.

Site: Bakersville, Mitchell County, North Carolina
metadata: Spruce Pine Quarry, located in Bakersville within North Carolina’s Spruce Pine Mining District, lies in the North Toe River valley of the Blue Ridge Mountains. The district produces mica, kaolin, feldspar, and the world’s largest concentration of high-purity quartz, supplying 70–90 percent of global demand for semiconductor and solar photovoltaic manufacturing. Ground mica is recovered during mineral extraction, while sheet mica remains unmined. As the hub of major mining activity, Spruce Pine is considered a critical point in the global supply chain, with analysts warning that disruption could halt integrated circuit production worldwide within six months.

Site: Durham Co., New South Wales
metadata: Rix’s Creek Open Cut Mine in Durham County, New South Wales, is part of the Integra Coal operation, 61.2 percent owned by Vale Australia with partners Posco and Toyota. Originally slated to close in 2020, the mine received approval in 2019 to operate until 2040 following multiple extensions. The New South Wales Environment Protection Authority has fined the mine for saline groundwater discharges from historical underground workings, which can disrupt aquatic ecosystems. Management measures, including borehole capping and pump reinstatement, have been implemented. In June 2025, a discharge occurred from an uncapped borehole drilled to assess old workings and improve seepage control.

Site: Gloucester Co., New South Wales
metadata: Duralie Coal Mine, also known as Stratford Duralie, is an open-cut operation in Gloucester County, New South Wales, with coal reserves of 322.3 million tonnes of coking coal, ranking among the largest in Asia and globally. The mine has an annual production capacity of five million tonnes. Coal extraction is scheduled to conclude in 2024, after which closure activities will include decommissioning infrastructure and rehabilitating the site to an approved final landform. Environmental monitoring and maintenance of rehabilitated areas are expected to continue for 10 to 15 years, with the New South Wales Government required to assess and approve closure works before mining leases can be relinquished

Site: Stratford open cut mine, Gloucester Co., New South Wales
metadata: Avon North Open Pit, part of the Stratford Mining Complex in the Gloucester Basin, New South Wales, is associated with the Stratford, Duralie, and Grant & Chainey deposits. While Stratford Mine ceased coal production in late 2021, Avon North may still have limited related activity. Stratford transitioned to closure in 2024, with bulk earthworks, reshaping, and topsoiling underway, and demolition of the coal handling and preparation plant commencing in May 2025 to safely remove hazardous materials, dismantle equipment, and dispose of foundations. Coal extraction at Duralie ended in December 2021, followed by rehandling, decommissioning, site rehabilitation, and ongoing environmental monitoring.

Site: Wambo Open Cut Mine, Hunter County, New South Wales
metadata: The United Wambo Open Cut Mine in the Hunter Valley, New South Wales, began production in 2020 as a 50:50 joint venture between United Collieries (Glencore and MEU) and Wambo Coal (Peabody). It integrates the former Wambo and United open-cut operations to produce thermal and semi-soft metallurgical coal for export. The mine has estimated reserves of 176 million tonnes, an annual production capacity of 10 million tonnes, an expected life of 23 years, and represents an investment of about $380 million. This development proceeds despite official forecasts of Australian thermal coal exports consistently overestimating demand and lacking scenarios that reflect urgent global commitments to reduce coal use.

Site: Collier Open Cut, Kennedy Co., New South Wales
metadata: Collier Open Cut, located near Condobolin in the Mineral Hill field of central New South Wales, Australia, was a major copper mining operation active during the early to mid-twentieth century. Initially developed as an open pit, it later transitioned to underground mining, reflecting evolving practices within the industry. The site formed part of a historically important district renowned for its rich and varied mineral deposits. Collier contributed significantly to the region’s mining output and development, with renewed activity and further development occurring in the 1940s and 1950s.

Site: Endeavour 22, Northparkes Mine Project, Goonumbla, Kennedy Co., New South Wales
metadata: Northparkes Mine near Goonumbla in central-west New South Wales is Australia’s second largest porphyry copper deposit after Cadia, with an endowment of about 5.5 million ounces of gold and 4.5 million tons of copper. Operated as a joint venture between Rio Tinto and Sumitomo Group, it produces high-grade copper-gold concentrate for international smelters. Exploration began in the early 1970s, leading to the discovery of multiple ore bodies, including Endeavour22. Northparkes pioneered block-cave underground mining in Australia in 1997 and currently operates two open pits and one underground mine within a belt of Ordovician volcanic rocks.

Site: Elliots No 1 Open cut, Captain's Flat, Lake George, Murray Co., New South Wales
metadata: Elliots No. 1 Open Cut at Captain’s Flat in the Lachlan Fold Belt of south-eastern New South Wales formed part of a major volcanic-hosted massive sulphide zinc-lead-copper deposit 50 kilometres south-east of Canberra. The ore comprised pyrite, sphalerite, galena, chalcopyrite, tennantite, arsenopyrite, gold and rare pyrrhotite with gangue of quartz, chert, carbonates, chlorite and sericite. Mining began after copper was discovered in 1874 and evolved through several companies and smelting operations. From 1882 to 1962 about 4 million tonnes of ore were extracted, yielding high grades of zinc, lead, copper, silver and gold. Prolonged miner strikes in 1948-49 and 1954-55 disrupted production before the mine finally closed in 1962.

Adi Rassi project, Dubaruba, Southern Region
metadata: The Adi Rassi deposit, 30 kilometres south of Asmara, occurs in the southern section of the Adi Rassi–Kodatu mineralised trend within the Asmara District. It contains an estimated resource of 16 million tonnes at 0.5 percent copper and 0.3 grams per tonne gold. Copper, gold and silver mineralisation is associated with foliation planes, breccia zones and quartz veins developed along a major north–south shear zone traceable for more than 3 kilometres. Mining at Adi Rassi began in the 1930s, when Italian operators excavated a series of 10 to 20 metre deep open pits along the shear zone, mainly targeting oxide copper in the South Hill zone. The deposit was again exploited briefly in the 1980s, but operations were halted by military conflict during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. Following renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000, modern exploration of the Asmara District resumed, focusing on volcanic-hosted massive sulphide copper, zinc, silver and gold systems and confirming Adi Rassi as a significant shear-hosted copper–gold target adjacent to the established volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits. These campaigns have progressively refined resources, geology and structural controls on mineralisation.

Site: Debarwa Mine, Dubaruba, Southern Region
metadata: The Debarwa deposit lies about 30 km south-southwest of central Asmara, the capital of Eritrea. It is hosted by an overturned, moderately to steeply dipping sequence of variably to intensely altered tholeiitic basaltic and rhyolitic volcanic rocks, with minor chemical sedimentary units. The exposed rusty, iron-rich, weathered caps at Debarwa attracted some of the earliest drilling in the district, both before and after Eritrean independence in 1993. The deposit was mined briefly in the 1980s, but operations ceased when military conflict intensified during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. After renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000 subsided, systematic exploration of the Asmara district resumed. Debarwa now forms part of the Asmara Project, an advanced copper-zinc-gold-silver development that includes four main deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Most operating and exploration companies involved are Western, particularly from Canada, Australia and the UK, and their senior technical expertise largely reflects these origins. Eritreans are employed mainly in supplies, accounting, clerical roles, machinery operation and technical support. However, the bulk of the manual workforce is drawn from National Service conscripts, who have been reported to work under highly exploitative conditions, with inadequate food and poor living quarters.

Site: Emba Derho Mine, Emba Derho, Central Region
metadata: Emba Derho is a large volcanogenic massive sulphide deposit hosting gold, silver, copper and zinc, located west-north-west of Asmara. It belongs to the Asmara Project, which comprises four advanced deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Mineralisation at Emba Derho extends over about 1,250 m of strike and 850 m in width, while Adi Nefas occurs as an elongated north-northeast-trending, steeply east-dipping massive sulphide layer within a bimodal sequence of metavolcanic and metasedimentary rocks. The project is planned in three phases. Phase I began with open-pit mining at Debarwa and shipment of direct shipping ore through the port of Massawa. Phase II adds a gold heap-leach operation on near-surface ore and mining of supergene copper from Debarwa and Emba Derho, processed at a central 2 million tonnes per year flotation plant at Emba Derho, with copper concentrate plus gold and silver by-products exported via Massawa. Phase III will treat primary copper and zinc ores from Emba Derho and nearby sites, producing copper and zinc concentrates. Power is generated onsite from diesel and medium-fuel-oil generators, water is largely recycled, yet local communities question the benefits and landscape and agricultural impacts.

Site: Gupo deposit, Ad Nefas, Asmara, Central Region
metadata: The Gupo mine lies within the Asmara District of Eritrea, where volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits of copper, zinc, silver and gold occur close to the capital, Asmara. Gupo itself is predominantly a gold deposit hosted in highly deformed, strongly foliated mafic flows and mafic tuffs, intercalated and crosscut by thin quartz-phyric felsic flows and subordinate dykes. The highest-grade gold mineralisation occurs in crystalline, coarse-grained pyrite within quartz veins, while lower-grade gold is associated with medium- to coarse-grained euhedral to sub-euhedral pyrite in sericite-altered selvages. Gupo forms part of the Asmara Project, held by the Asmara Mining Share Company, a 60:40 joint venture between the Chinese company SRBM and state-owned ENAMCO. The project comprises four deposits—Emba Derho, Adi Nefas, Gupo and Debarwa. In the first phase, high-grade copper ore will be mined by open pit at Debarwa, containerised, and transported 120 km to the port of Massawa for shipment to a Chinese smelter. At full production, the project is expected to operate for 17 years and annually yield about 29,000 t copper, 83,000 t zinc, 42,000 oz gold and 1 million oz silver in its first eight years. Concerns have been voiced about the benefit of these operations to the local inhabitants and the impact on the surrounding landscape and agriculture.

Site: Hambok Zn-Ag Project, Gash Barka Region
metadata: The Hambok mine, a major silver- and zinc-bearing site, is one of several operations developed along Eritrea’s volcanic massive sulfide belt, where mineral extraction has a long history. After a suspension of all mineral exploration was lifted in early 2005, the Government revised its mining framework, increasing its maximum equity stake in projects to 30% from 2006 onward. Eritrea’s mining code, modelled on that of Australia’s Northern Territory, stipulates royalties of 5% on precious metals and 3% on base metals, alongside a 35% corporate tax rate. Within this framework, Sanu Resources emerged as the leading exploration landholder, with licences covering more than 2,600 km², focused largely on Hambok, its most recent volcanic massive sulfide discovery in the polymetallic belt. Drilling—7,382 m across 43 diamond drill holes—confirmed extensive massive sulfide mineralisation over more than 1,050 m of strike length and 300 m downdip, remaining open in all directions and indicating significant copper-zinc potential. Since 2012, the Hambok copper-zinc deposit has been part of the Bisha Mining Company, 60% owned by Nevsun Resources and 40% by the Eritrean National Mining Company.

Site: Harena Mine, Gash Barka Region
metadata: The Harena Mine is an open copper and zinc operation located close to the better-known Bisha site in Eritrea’s Gash-Barka region, north of the provincial capital Barentu. Developed as a satellite deposit to the northwest of the Bisha main zone, Harena is estimated, as of 2023, to contain proven and probable reserves of about 3.09 million tonnes of zinc at an average grade of 3.75% and 800,000 tonnes of copper at 0.97%. The operation is run by Zijin-Bisha Mining, an Eritrean company established to develop and operate the Bisha district mines, and jointly owned by China’s Zijin Mining Group, with a 55% stake, and the state-owned Eritrean National Mining Corporation, with 45%. Although the region is highly mineralised, it is also the agricultural heartland of Eritrea and was once heavily forested before widespread deforestation transformed the highland plateau. Politically, the country has been ruled by a single party, the People’s Front for Democracy and Justice, the successor to the Eritrean People’s Liberation Front, since independence in 1991, and continues to operate under a transitional government elected in 1993, with national elections originally scheduled for 2001 repeatedly postponed. This political context shapes governance of mining and land use practices.

Site: Ugaro Mine, Awgaro (Ugaro), Gash Barka Region
metadata: The south-western part of Eritrea is one of the most prospective gold-bearing regions in the country. The Augaro mine developed on a series of quartz veins and stringers, some showing sulphide mineralisation, formed in a low-pressure zone related to a regional shear system. The wider area hosts hundreds of small historic mines and operations that were active during the Italian occupation, which ended in 1941 with the East African campaign of World War II. Augaro was reportedly the most productive gold mine in Eritrea, and during Italian colonisation mercury amalgamation was widely used, with tailings dumped without proper waste management. Today, gold mining is increasingly informal and largely outside federal control; some projects have been overtaken by illegal exploration, extraction and smuggling, often in collusion with state and non-state actors. These activities generally ignore sustainable mining practices and worker health. A major environmental and health concern is mercury release from artisanal and small-scale mining, including in-situ evaporation from tailings near farming areas in Eritrea’s hot climate. Land degradation from unrehabilitated pits is substantial, and because small-scale mining is largely informal and miners’ awareness is limited, land reclamation remains extremely rare.
